findings.issue: Stylistic: initialize() uses a single-line `withContext(Dispatchers.Default) { lifecycleMutex.withLock {` opener with a matching `} }` closer on line 188, while cleanup() (lines 190-198) and resetConversation() (lines 200-209) expand the two blocks onto separate lines.
findings.impact: Inconsistent visual shape between the three lifecycle methods; the `} }` closer of initialize is easy to misread as a typo even though brace pairing is correct. Expression body (`=`) is preserved, so the transformation is semantics-equivalent.
findings.recommendation: Either (a) convert initialize to a block body with an explicit `return` so it mirrors cleanup/resetConversation's shape, or (b) leave as-is and accept the visual asymmetry. Not blocking.

summary: Fix is correct and production-safe. Dispatchers.Default is the right pool: LlmChatModelHelper.initialize is a synchronous CPU- and native-bound call that warms the LiteRT GPU backend — not file I/O — so Default (CPU-bound pool sized to core count) fits better than IO (elastic pool for blocking I/O). Brace pairing on initialize is correct: the single-expression body `= withContext(Dispatchers.Default) { lifecycleMutex.withLock { ... } }` closes with matching `} }` on line 188, and every `return@withLock` site returns `Result<Unit>`, which propagates through withContext back to the suspend function return. lifecycleMutex semantics are unchanged — the withContext hop happens before acquiring the mutex, so every lifecycle transition is still fully serialized under the same Mutex, and the mutex holder executes on Default throughout (suspending points inside withLock such as awaitInitialize keep the mutex held exactly as before). StateFlow emissions from updateEntry (line 161, 168, 181, 186, 195) are safe from non-Main threads — MutableStateFlow.update is thread-safe and StateFlow collectors in Compose (via collectAsStateWithLifecycle) marshal to Main themselves. suspendCancellableCoroutine.invokeOnCancellation (lines 255-258) still fires correctly: cancellation of the outer coroutine propagates through withContext and withLock into the suspension point, and the registered handler runs on the cancelling dispatcher (no dispatcher requirement on invokeOnCancellation). The cleanup path from ChatViewModel.onCleared (ChatViewModel.kt:140) launches on `CoroutineScope(SupervisorJob() + Dispatchers.IO)` and then hops to Default inside the registry — the extra hop is a single coroutine continuation dispatch, harmless and worth the consistency of keeping dispatcher choice inside the registry (callers shouldn't need to know which pool fits the underlying native work). One stylistic inconsistency noted (initialize's one-line withContext+withLock opener vs cleanup/resetConversation's expanded form) — not blocking. No critical or major findings; approve.
